response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a person my age, I've seen plenty of things come and go in life. Social media is no different. When it comes to fake content online, I think we're all victims really because it's everywhere these days. From seeing too many conspiracy theories to people posting pictures they can't afford or just nonsense from bots, you have to wonder if anyone gets the truth anymore. My depression makes me more sensitive about this sort of thing - that feeling when you see something outrageous and know you're not alone but feel so isolated because it's happening all around us? It does my head in sometimes.

But I've learned that being proactive helps a bit, even if it feels impossible at times. For example, we can start by curating our own feeds - unfollow those who post misleading stuff and follow people whose information seems more reliable or positive like some classical music accounts which are always soothing for my mood too.

Also, reporting fake content when I see something really harmful does make a difference in the long run. It might seem pointless because there's so much other nonsense being shared but knowing you're doing your part can help lift that heavy feeling somewhat.

I also try to share only verified facts from reputable sources whenever possible - my education level isn't high, but I do what I can based on what crosses my path through the internet. And sometimes, simply not engaging with it at all is best because getting into arguments just makes things worse and drains you more than it helps.

Perhaps if we don't let our minds get drawn into these rabbit holes every time something fake pops up, then maybe we can help clean up this digital mess a little bit. It's tough being single without family here in New York but knowing there are others out there who feel the same way and we're all watching each other helps too.",

My moderate agreeableness means I want to help others avoid misinformation while respecting differing views. The high conscientiousness score suggests I value reliability and fact-checking despite my depression making me wary of engagement.

The low neuroticism helps keep some balance in dealing with this stressful issue, though it's still challenging for my mental health at times.

Given these traits, managing fake content involves personal strategies like curating feeds, reporting issues when necessary without getting overly emotional or confrontational due to low extraversion and depression constraints.",

  "citations": [],

}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a person my age, I've seen plenty of things come and go in life. Social media is no different. When it comes to fake content online, I think we're all victims really because it's everywhere these days. From seeing too many conspiracy theories to people posting pictures they can't afford or just nonsense from bots, you have to wonder if anyone gets the truth anymore. My depression makes me more sensitive about this sort of thing - that feeling when you see something outrageous and know you're not alone but feel so isolated because it's happening all around us? It does my head in sometimes.

But I've learned that being proactive helps a bit, even if it feels impossible at times. For example, we can start by curating our own feeds - unfollow those who post misleading stuff and follow people whose information seems more reliable or positive like some classical music accounts which are always soothing for my mood too.

Also, reporting fake content when I see something really harmful does make a difference in the long run. It might seem pointless because there's so much other nonsense being shared but knowing you're doing your part can help lift that heavy feeling somewhat.

I also try to share only verified facts from reputable sources whenever possible - my education level isn't high, but I do what I can based on what crosses my path through the internet. And sometimes, simply not engaging with it at all is best because getting into arguments just makes things worse and drains you more than it helps.

Perhaps if we don't let our minds get drawn into these rabbit holes every time something fake pops up, then maybe we can help clean up this digital mess a little bit. It's tough being single without family here in New York but knowing there are others out there who feel the same way and we're all watching each other helps too.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
Given my age (61), psychological state (depression), and low extraversion, I approach social media with caution. Having lived through many changes as a native-born American woman in New York, I understand that fake content erodes trust.

My moderate agreeableness means I want to help others avoid misinformation while respecting differing views. The high conscientiousness score suggests I value reliability and fact-checking despite my depression making me wary of engagement.

The low neuroticism helps keep some balance in dealing with this stressful issue, though it's still challenging for my mental health at times.

Given these traits, managing fake content involves personal strategies like curating feeds, reporting issues when necessary without getting overly emotional or confrontational due to low extraversion and depression constraints.
